SOC 245 · Research Paper · April 2026

Soft Nostalgia
and the New Indochine

A Visual and Spatial Analysis of Feminine Aesthetics
in Vietnamese Gen Z Fashion vs. Manhattan Luxury Brands

Felicity (Hang) Le  ·  Prof. VoonChin Phua

scroll
Introduction Theory Methods Definitions Findings Gallery Conclusion Last Words References
Introduction

Fashion as a Social Text

Fashion, at its core, is a silent language and a narrative of the land from which it is born. Vietnam celebrated only fifty years of full independence last year. For a nation that young, identity is not a settled question. It is something actively being constructed, contested, and claimed, especially by a generation that grew up after the war but carries its inheritance. For Vietnamese people, identity is not incidental to daily life. It is central to it.

At the heart of this evolution is the New Indochine aesthetic, pioneered by local labels such as Dear Jose, Onon Made, and Fanci Club. These designers were not handed a centuries-old fashion institution to inherit. They built their aesthetic from the ground up, with bold ideas, niche sensibilities, and a pride in materiality felt the moment a garment is touched.

This research asks one question: how does the aesthetic of soft femininity operate differently across cultural contexts, and what does that difference reveal about identity, memory, and postcolonial self-expression? It matters because it documents a process happening right now, at a specific and unrepeatable moment. A fifty-year-old independent nation is finding its visual voice, and young designers are leading the way.

“In New York, softness decorates. In Vietnam, it declares.”

If the designs emerging from Manhattan speak the language of luxury and spectacle, then the softness of the New Indochine reflects something altogether quieter: the silt of the Red and Mekong Rivers, patient and deeply nostalgic.

9Brands Studied6 Vietnamese · 3 Manhattan
55Images AnalyzedDigital & in-person fieldwork
202Coded Segmentsavg. 3.67 codes per image
Literature Review

Theoretical Framework

The theoretical framework for this study is organized around three interlocking themes: the politics of cultural identity in dress, the role of memory and inheritance in aesthetic practice, and the spatial and visual grammar through which femininity is communicated and consumed.

Identity and the Dressed Body

Fashion has long been understood as a social text, a system of signs through which individuals and communities communicate who they are and what they believe the world owes them the right to express. Diana Crane (2000) argues that clothing is one of the most immediate tools through which class, gender, and cultural identity are constructed and contested. To wear something is to make a claim. Joanne Entwistle (2000) extends this by arguing that dress is not merely a visual practice but an embodied one. The weight of silk against the skin, the precision of a cut, the quiet restraint of muted embroidery: these are the vocabulary of a fashion culture that understands clothing as something deeply personal rather than merely decorative.

Memory, Hybridity, and Postcolonial Reclamation

To understand the cultural logic of the New Indochine, one must grapple with the concept of hybridity as theorized by Homi Bhabha (1994). Bhabha argues that postcolonial cultures do not simply absorb or reject the aesthetic inheritance of their colonizers. Instead they inhabit an ambivalent creative space where inherited symbols are quietly transformed and reauthored into something that belongs to no one but themselves. The visual language of French Indochine, its soft silhouettes, lace details, muted florals, and delicate draping, was historically imposed as part of a broader project of cultural domination. What Vietnamese Gen Z designers are doing now is taking that same vocabulary and speaking something entirely new with it. The result is not nostalgia for a colonial past but a reclamation of aesthetic territory, worn with confidence rather than grief.

The generational relationship to this history is illuminated by Marianne Hirsch’s (2012) concept of postmemory, which describes how younger generations engage deeply with formative experiences they did not personally live through. For Vietnamese Gen Z, the colonial and wartime periods are not living memories but inherited ones, transmitted through family stories, cultural imagery, and the built environment of cities like Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City. As Russell Belk (1990) observes, material objects function as anchors of both personal and collective memory. A dress, in this reading, can hold an entire history.

The Spatial and Visual Grammar of Femininity

Erving Goffman (1979) demonstrated that the spatial and visual arrangements of fashion, store interiors, lookbook photography, the careful art direction of an Instagram grid, instruct consumers in how they are meant to feel as gendered subjects. This instruction is never neutral. Van Leeuwen and Jewitt (2001) provide the methodological foundation for reading these arrangements as structured visual texts, amenable to systematic analysis. Together these frameworks make it possible to ask not just what Vietnamese and Manhattan brands look like, but what they are saying, and to whom.

Bhabha (1994)
Cultural Hybridity

Postcolonial cultures inhabit an ambivalent creative space where inherited symbols are quietly transformed and reauthored into something that belongs to no one but themselves.

Hirsch (2012)
Postmemory

Younger generations engage deeply with formative experiences they did not personally live through. For Vietnamese Gen Z, colonial history is carried in family, cultural imagery, and built environment.

Entwistle (2000)
Embodied Dress

Dress is not merely a visual practice but an embodied one. The weight of silk against the skin is identity-work.

Goffman (1979)
Spatial Gaze

Store interiors, lookbook photography, and Instagram grids instruct consumers in how they are meant to feel as gendered subjects. This instruction is never neutral.

Belk (1990)
Object-Memory

Material objects function as anchors of both personal and collective memory. A dress can hold an entire history.

Crane (2000)
Fashion as Social Text

Clothing is one of the most immediate tools through which class, gender, and cultural identity are constructed and contested. To wear something is to make a claim.

Data and Methods

Two Worlds of Soft Femininity

This study employs a multi-site purposive sampling method to compare two distinct geographical centers of the soft feminine aesthetic. The total sample consists of nine brands, six Vietnamese and three American, alongside fifty-five images gathered across both digital and physical research environments.

Vietnam

New Indochine Aesthetic

  • Onon Made
  • Fanci Club
  • Dear Jose
  • Aeie
  • Lsoul

“In Vietnam, softness declares.”

Manhattan

American Luxury Romantic

  • LoveShackFancy · Nolita
  • Kate Spade · SoHo
  • Retrofete · SoHo

“In New York, softness decorates.”

The analytic approach weaves together Qualitative Content Analysis and Semiotic Analysis, treating both Instagram images and store environments as texts that can be read for deeper meaning. All 55 images were coded in MAXQDA using six operationally defined codes in two passes for consistency. Three primary dimensions guide the coding process: materiality, which attends to how specific fabrics function as carriers of cultural and emotional meaning; symbolism, which examines how vintage French and Indochine motifs operate differently in New York and Saigon; and the spatial gaze, which considers how the physical arrangement of a store or the curated sequence of an Instagram grid teaches consumers how to inhabit their femininity.

Ethical Considerations

This study required ethical care at every stage of data collection. The digital images analyzed are drawn exclusively from publicly accessible business accounts and official brand websites, in keeping with established best practices for internet research ethics (Zimmer 2010). No private individual data was collected or used. For the Manhattan fieldwork, all participants took part voluntarily and were informed in advance that their responses would contribute to academic research. No sensitive personal information was solicited or recorded. The use of brand imagery for academic analysis constitutes fair scholarly commentary and does not require commercial licensing.

On the Use of MAXQDA

This study used MAXQDA, a qualitative data analysis software, as the primary tool for coding the image corpus. The advantages of using MAXQDA for this kind of visual research are significant. First, it allows the researcher to draw segments directly onto an image and attach code labels to specific regions, meaning the analysis is spatially precise rather than simply impressionistic. A reader can see exactly which part of a photograph earned a particular code, which strengthens transparency and reproducibility. Second, MAXQDA supports multiple code passes on the same image, which allowed this study to conduct two independent coding rounds and compare results for consistency. Third, the software generates frequency reports automatically, making it straightforward to count how many times each code appeared across the full corpus without manual tallying. The limitation of using MAXQDA for image analysis, compared to numerical data processing, is primarily computational. Image files are substantially larger than text or numerical datasets, and a corpus of fifty-five high-resolution photographs creates a heavier storage and processing load than most researchers anticipate at the outset. There is also a learning curve: coding images in MAXQDA requires more setup time than coding text, because each image must be imported individually and the segment drawing tools behave differently from text highlighting. These are worth noting for future researchers who wish to replicate or extend this approach at larger scale.

Definitions

MAXQDA Coding Framework

Table 1 presents the six codes used in this study, organized by dimension. Each code is defined by the visual indicators used to apply it consistently across the full image corpus of 55 photographs.

Table 1. MAXQDA Coding Framework: Six Codes, Dimensions, Frequencies, and Visual Indicators

CodeDim.n%Visual Indicators
Styling FemininityFemininity6632.7Floral embroidery; lace trim; soft draping; fitted waist; flowing skirt
Material SoftnessSoftness6230.7Silk, chiffon, lace, organza, sheer layers; draping, fluid movement
Visual SoftnessSoftness2411.9Muted/pastel palette; diffused light; floral motifs; curved composition
Western RomanticCulture19 9.4Saturated maximalist floral; sequins; cottage setting; performative glamour
Indochine MotifCulture18 8.9Ao dai collar or silhouette; fitted column with side slit; French-influenced lace; organza bow neckline
Spatial FemininityFemininity13 6.4Floral props; soft/candlelit lighting; pastel interiors; gentle poses

Total: 202 coded segments · 55 images · avg. 3.67 codes per image

Results and Discussion

What the Codes Reveal

Before drawing conclusions, it is necessary to show what was actually found in the images, brand by brand, code by code. The six codes fall into three paired dimensions. Material Softness and Visual Softness together measure the aesthetic register of softness. Styling Femininity and Spatial Femininity together measure how femininity is expressed at the level of the garment and the environment. Indochine Motif and Western Romantic are mutually exclusive cultural codes that capture the distinct visual traditions each national context draws upon.

A note for readers unfamiliar with the Indochine Motif code: this code was applied when an image contained specific visual references to Vietnamese colonial era dress culture. These include the high mandarin collar and vertical button closure associated with the ao dai, Vietnam’s traditional formal garment; the fitted column silhouette with a side slit that is the ao dai’s defining shape; gathered or ruched necklines tied with a bow, which reference the tied collar of the ao dai; and structured puff sleeves combined with a corseted waist, which echo Indochine era Vietnamese formal dress construction. These are not generic feminine details. They are specific to a visual tradition that is recognizable to Vietnamese audiences as a form of cultural continuity, and to international audiences as a deliberate aesthetic choice rooted in a particular history.

Vietnamese Brands

Aeie presented the most visually confrontational aesthetic in the Vietnamese sample. All eight images received Material Softness and Styling Femininity codes, confirming that soft fabrics and feminine garment construction are consistently present. However, Visual Softness was absent in nearly all images. Aeie shoots in dark, dramatic lighting against deep backgrounds, presenting silk and lace in an atmosphere that is anything but gentle. This is a deliberate tension: the materials are soft but the visual world they inhabit is hard. No Indochine Motif codes were applied to Aeie. The brand appears to be operating in a globally oriented avant-garde register rather than drawing on specifically Vietnamese visual references.

Dear Jose offered the clearest and most concentrated examples of the Indochine Motif in the entire corpus. All seven images received Material Softness and Styling Femininity codes. The Indochine Motif appeared in five of seven images, coded specifically at the high ruched neckline with organza bow detail, which references the tied ao dai collar; at the fitted corseted waist with puff sleeves, which echoes Indochine era Vietnamese formal dress; and at the overall silhouette, which mirrors the ao dai’s column form. Visual Softness appeared in four images, all of which used warm terracotta tones and diffused lighting. Dear Jose is the brand in this study that most completely integrates material softness, styling femininity, and Indochine cultural memory into a single coherent visual argument.

Fanci Club was the largest sample in the Vietnamese corpus at fifteen images, and it revealed the most internal variation. Material Softness and Styling Femininity were present across all fifteen images. The Indochine Motif appeared in five images, coded at a rose choker referencing the traditional Vietnamese flower choker worn with ao dai, at embroidered details echoing Vietnamese craft traditions, and at garment silhouettes with high neck references. Visual Softness appeared in only four images, suggesting that Fanci Club, like Aeie, often presents soft materials within a visually bold or high contrast atmosphere.

Lsoul produced eight images that collectively scored highest on Spatial Femininity among all Vietnamese brands, with the code appearing in four images. These were the only Vietnamese brand images in the corpus to place models in soft outdoor environments, including a misty garden setting and a flower-lined path. The Indochine Motif appeared in two images, coded at the fitted column silhouette with side slit referencing the ao dai form, and at the warm studio backdrop combined with a fitted dress that echoes Indochine era portrait photography.

Onon Made was the most consistently coded brand in the entire corpus. All seven images received Material Softness, Visual Softness, and Styling Femininity codes simultaneously, making it the only brand where all three codes co-occurred in every image. The Indochine Motif appeared in five of seven images, coded at a sailor hat referencing French colonial Vietnamese dress codes, at shell and rattan accessories referencing Vietnamese coastal craft traditions, and at botanical lace patterns echoing Vietnamese embroidery traditions and the French broderie anglaise adopted into Vietnamese formal dress during the colonial period.

Manhattan Brands

LoveShackFancy produced five images that scored highest on Western Romantic in the Manhattan sample, with the code appearing in all five images. Three images also received Spatial Femininity codes, making LoveShackFancy the brand with the most consistent environmental femininity in the entire corpus. These images place models in lush garden settings filled with blooming flowers, using pastel lighting and abundant floral props to create an immersive pastoral atmosphere. No Indochine Motif codes were applied to any LoveShackFancy image.

Kate Spade produced five images that received Material Softness, Styling Femininity, and Western Romantic codes across all five. The Western Romantic code was applied at bold saturated floral embroidery in bright rather than muted tones, at maximalist garment construction with heavy surface embellishment, and at the overall performative confidence of the styling, which communicates joy and glamour rather than quietness or restraint. No Indochine Motif codes were applied.

Retrofete produced four images all receiving Material Softness, Styling Femininity, and Western Romantic codes. The brand’s aesthetic is the most evening wear oriented in the Manhattan sample, with sequined and heavily embellished garments that communicate celebration and spectacle. No Indochine Motif codes were applied.

Three Core Findings

01The Shared Surface

Material Softness (62) and Styling Femininity (66) together account for 63% of all coded segments. The surface vocabulary of soft femininity is genuinely cross-cultural and shared across both nations.

02The Atmospheric Divide

Visual Softness (24) and Spatial Femininity (13) split sharply by nation. Manhattan brands score high on both. Vietnamese avant-garde brands refuse the soft atmosphere while embracing soft materials.

03Mirror Image Cultural Codes

Western Romantic (19) and Indochine Motif (18) appear at near-identical frequencies but in completely opposite national contexts. Not a single image received both codes. They are mutually exclusive.

Taken together, these findings support and extend the theoretical frameworks established in the literature review. Bhabha’s concept of hybridity is visible in the Indochine Motif codes: Vietnamese designers are not imitating colonial aesthetics nor rejecting them, but transforming them into something that belongs entirely to the present. Hirsch’s postmemory is legible in the specific details that earned Indochine Motif codes, the ao dai collar, the fitted slit silhouette, the French influenced lace, all of which are inherited visual forms being reauthored by a generation that did not live through the colonial period but carries its memory. Goffman’s spatial gaze explains why the Atmospheric Divide matters: the choice to place soft garments in a hard visual world, as Aeie and Fanci Club do, is itself a gendered statement, a refusal to perform the gentle passivity that soft materials might otherwise imply.

Image Corpus

Photo Gallery

All 55 images from the study, organized by brand or by MAXQDA code. Hover over any image to see its codes. Click to enlarge.

Conclusion

What a Post-War Nation Wears

This study set out to ask what softness means when it crosses national borders, and the answer it found is that the word covers two very different things. At the surface level, both Vietnamese and Manhattan brands are soft. Both reach for lace and silk and floral motifs and the feminine silhouette. But the atmosphere in which that softness lives, and the cultural memory it carries, could not be more different.

For LoveShackFancy or Kate Spade, softness is pastoral fantasy. The nostalgia is beautiful but it is not weighted. For Dear Jose or Onon Made, softness is a position. It is a generation that inherited a colonial visual vocabulary and chose to reclaim it rather than discard it, reauthoring its aesthetic language as an act of cultural sovereignty.

Softness, defined precisely and measured consistently, tells us something true about who a culture believes it is. This generation of Vietnamese designers did not inherit a fashion institution. They built one, from silk chosen carefully and embroidery that means something.

Future research would benefit from a larger image corpus and the inclusion of consumer perspectives, asking not only what brands communicate but how audiences in Vietnam and abroad receive and interpret these visual arguments. There is also rich comparative work to be done with other postcolonial fashion scenes in Southeast Asia.

Last Words

What I Learned and Unlearned

I want to begin this section by expressing genuine gratitude. Professor Phua’s guidance shaped not only the direction of this research but the way I learned to think inside it, to slow down, to be precise, to ask what I actually mean when I use a word like soft. I am also grateful to the Sociology Department at Gettysburg College, whose support made the site visits to Manhattan possible. Walking into LoveShackFancy, Kate Spade, and Retrofete, physically standing in those spaces and feeling how the lighting and the furniture and the arrangement of garments created an atmosphere, gave me something no Instagram scroll could have. That experience is woven into every observation in this paper.

What I learned most unexpectedly in SOC 245 is that images are everywhere and yet almost nowhere in research. We are surrounded by photographs, on our phones, on our feeds, in the stores we walk through, and they are shaping how we understand gender, beauty, and culture in ways most people never consciously register. Learning to treat a photograph as a primary data source, to read it with the same rigor and care that a researcher brings to a survey or an interview, felt like being handed a new way of seeing. I did not expect that to feel so significant, but it did.

I also want to say something about what I hope comes next, not just for my own work, but for the field. There is still a widespread assumption that images are soft data, too impressionistic, too easily manipulated to be taken seriously alongside numbers. But numbers can be manipulated too. Every dataset reflects the choices of the person who designed it. What I hope this paper contributes to, in however small a way, is a conversation about standardizing and legitimizing image-based research, developing shared protocols so that visual analysis can be recognized as the rigorous, replicable, scientific practice it is capable of being. The images are already there. We just need to agree that they are worth reading carefully.
